alsoKnownAs: Anubias barteri var. nana × A. gilletii

nativeRange.habitat: Cultivated hybrid not found in the wild; parent species (Anubias barteri var. nana and A. gilletii) are native to shaded streams, swamps, and forest margins of West and Central Africa, where they typically grow epiphytically on wood and rock at or just below the water line

decorPreferences: driftwood or rock for rhizome attachment
shaded or moderately lit placement to limit leaf algae
hardscape that lifts the rhizome above the substrate

avoidWith: large herbivorous cichlids that uproot hardscape

summary: A slow-growing Anubias cultivar with long, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves. Generally considered an undemanding beginner plant; the rhizome must be attached to hardscape rather than buried in substrate.
careNotes:
Anubias 'Nangi' is a cultivated hybrid rather than a wild species, believed to have been developed by plant breeder Robert A. Gasser and in trade since the mid-1980s. Visually it sits between its parent species: clearly larger and longer-leaved than Anubias barteri var. nana, but markedly smaller than A. heterophylla, with narrow, slightly bullous (lightly puckered), lance-shaped leaves typically 8–15 cm long. Submerged plants generally reach 10–20 cm tall; emersed nursery growth can reach 25–30 cm.

Husbandry follows the standard Anubias approach. Low to medium lighting is preferred — strong light combined with the plant's naturally slow growth tends to encourage algae on the leaves. CO2 supplementation is optional rather than required, and growth will be slow under all conditions regardless.

**DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME.** The rhizome — the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge — must be kept above the substrate. Burial causes it to rot. Instead, tie or super-glue the rhizome to driftwood, rock, or other hardscape and allow the roots to anchor themselves over time. As an epiphyte, Anubias 'Nangi' draws nutrients primarily from the water column, so liquid fertilization is more useful than substrate fertilization.

Reported tolerances are wide (12–30 °C, pH 5–8, kH 0–15, gH 0–25); the comfortable mid-range for most planted tanks is 22–28 °C and pH 6.0–7.8. Its tough, leathery leaves tend to resist the nibbling and uprooting that would damage softer plants, making it a practical choice for tanks housing African cichlids or goldfish.
breedingNotes: Propagated by rhizome division: with a clean sharp blade, cut the rhizome into sections, ensuring each section retains at least 3 healthy leaves and some attached roots, then re-attach each section to hardscape without burying it. Daughter plants that form along the rhizome can also be cut off and replanted. Like other Anubias, it can flower underwater, producing the small spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of Araceae, but seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium and (as a hybrid cultivar) would not breed true.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Confirmed common name, easy/beginner care, low-light tolerance, slow growth, epiphytic placement (attach to rock/driftwood), pointy long leaves, hardy leaves suitable for African cichlid and goldfish tanks, and propagation via horizontal rhizome. Product page did not state numerical water parameters or hybrid parentage.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 404 — product page no longer exists. Walked down the fallback chain per playbook (Modern Aquarium also 404'd, KFK Aquatics connection refused, Aquarium Plants Factory search returned no Nangi hit) before landing on Aquasabi and Flowgrow as effective secondaries.

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Effective secondary after primary-source fallback chain exhausted. Confirmed cultivar status, hybrid parentage Anubias barteri var. nana × A. gilletii (attributed to Robert A. Gasser, in trade since 1986), temperature 12–30 °C, pH 5–8, gH 0–30, kH 0–15, submerged height 5–20 cm (emersed up to 30 cm), very easy care, low light tolerated, CO2 not required, slow growth, epiphytic placement, rhizome-division propagation.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Effective tertiary. Confirmed taxonomy (Alismatales / Araceae), hybrid parentage A. barteri var. nana × A. gilletii, temperature 12–30 °C (optimal 24–28 °C), pH 5–8, kH 0–15, submerged height 5–15 cm, very easy care, slow growth, lighting low–high, epiphyte/midground/foreground/nano placement, propagation by rhizome division or daughter plants. Notes wide-lanceolate, slightly bullous, dark-green leaves; described as a 'cichlid-proof' plant.
